title: Teacher Sense of Efficacy Scale Short Form
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"4934l","text":"Teachers’ Sense  of Efficacy is the beliefs in their capability to make a difference in  student learning, to be able to get through even to students who are  difficult or unmotivated","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1162l","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f19oc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"43h6h","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fkn5s","text":"d. The Teacher Sense of Efficacy Scale asks  teachers to assess their capability concerning instructional strategies,  student engagement, and classroom management.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"69d2m","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1d0hd","text":"This protocol taken from Megan Tschannen-Moran's classic work on Teacher Self-Efficacy.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":25,"length":22},{"style":"italic","offset":65,"length":21}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"bve43","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b4v4g","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"6u4rf","text":"This questionnaire is designed to help us gain a better understanding of the kinds of things that create challenges for teachers. Your answers are confidential.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"4l9io","text":"Please indicate your opinion about each of the questions below by marking any one of the nine responses in the columns on the right side, ranging from (1) “None at all” to (9) “A Great Deal” as each represents a degree on the continuum.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"8oapr","text":"Please respond to each of the questions by considering the combination of your current ability, resources, and opportunity to do each of the following in your present position. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: 00:02:00 How much can you do to control disruptive behavior in the classroom? <imagine all of these being likert scale items instead of random reagents and readings> Sodium bicarbonate Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #S6014 Ascorbic acid P212121
How much can you do to motivate students who show low interest in school work? 212 °C 00:00:00
How much can you do to calm a student who is disruptive or noisy? BUG (for social science use): GOTO does not allow you to skip, only to go back. Also, no highlighting text.
How much can you do to help your students value learning?
To what extent can you craft good questions for your students?
How much can you do to get children to follow classroom rules?
How much can you do to get students to believe they can do well in school work?
How well can you establish a classroom management system with each group of students?
To what extent can you use a variety of assessment strategies?
To what extent can you provide an alternative explanation or example when students are confused?
How much can you assist families in helping their children do well in school?
How well can you implement alternative teaching strategies in your classroom?
